0: Environmental evaluation of transfer and treatment of excess pig slurry by life cycle assessment
Santiago Lopez-Ridaura a,b, Hayo van der Werfa, Jean Marie Paillat Bertrand Le Bris d

A R T I INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 11 January 2007 Received in revised form 4 July 2008 Accepted 27 July 2008 Available online 14 September 2008 Keywords: Slurry management Environmental assessment Life cycle assessment Gaseous emissions Slurry treatment Slurry transfer Livestock manure|Slurry management is a central topic in the agronomic and environmental analysis of intensive livestock production systems. The objective of this study is to compare the environmental performance of two scenarios of collective slurry management for the disposal of excess nitrogen from animal manure. The scenarios are the transfer of slurry and its injection to crop land, and the treatment of slurry in a collective biological treatment station. The study is based on a real case in the West of France, where a group of farmers is developing a collective plan for the disposal of almost 7000 m³ of excess pig slurry. The evaluation is carried out by Life Cycle Assessment, where emissions and resource consumption are quantified and aggregated into four environmental impact categories: eutrophication, acidification, climate change, and non-renewable energy use. Ammonia emitted is the most important contributor to acidification and eutrophication, while methane contributes most to climate change. Both ammonia and methane are mostly emitted during the storage of slurry and, in the case of the treatment scenario, also during composting the solid fraction of the slurry. The two management strategies are similar with respect to climate change, whereas eutrophi- cation and acidification are twice as large for treatment relative to transfer. Electricity needed for the treatment process is the main contributor to non-renewable energy use for the treatment scenario, while the transfer scenario represents a net energy saving, as energy saved by the reduction of mineral fertiliser use more than compensates for the energy needed for transport and injection of slurry. The overall environmental performance of transfer is better than that of treatment, as it involves less acidification, eutrophication and non-renewable energy use. The method employed and the results obtained in this study can provide elements for a transparent discussion of the advantages and disadvantages of contrasting excess slurry management scenarios as well as the identification of the main aspects determining their environmental performance. © 2008 Elsevier Ltd. 1: Livestock production makes an important contribution to most economies. In Europe, livestock commodities represent the highest value of agricultural production for most countries (FAO, 2006). The intensification of livestock production in the last decades has been accompanied by its dissociation from crop production, as it substantially relies on imported feed for its economic profitability. Such imports have generated new challenges related to the treat- ment and disposal of manure and slurry, as increased nutrient concentrations on crop fields and in underground and surface

water threaten the ecological stability of regions where intensive livestock production takes place. Moreover, gaseous emissions (NH3, N20 and CH4), resulting from animal buildings, manure and slurry storage and spreading on crop land, also represent an important environmental burden associated with intensive live- stock production. CH4 and N20 are powerful greenhouse gases (Kroeze, 1994; Houghton et al., 2001). NH3 is responsible for acid- ification of rain and of the environment and for the formation of aerosols (ApSimon et al., 1987; Fangmeier et al., 1994), it also contributes indirectly to N20 emission by soils (IPCC, 2006). Bretagne, in the West of France, concentrates 40% of the coun- try's intensive livestock farming, producing 56% of the country's pigs, 31% of its poultry and 21% of its dairy products (Savelli and Cebron, 2006). Intensive livestock production has also positioned Bretagne as one of the most polluted regions in France, especially

with respect to nitrate in ground and surface water, as organic and mineral Nitrogen (N) applied largely surpass crop needs. It has been calculated that as much as 103,000 tons of N (30% of the total N applied) are applied in excess of crop needs, 65% of which come from animal manure (Cebron and Ferron, 2003).

As part of the implementation of policies related to the Nitrate Directive of the European Union (91/676/CEE), districts (cantons) in France have been classified in relation to their vulnerability to water pollution by N used in agriculture. Structural Surplus Zones (Zones d'Excedent Structurel -ZES-) have been defined as zones where the production of N in the form of animal manure surpasses a threshold of 170 kg ha per year over the spreadable area. In Bretagne, 104 out of 187 cantons are classified as ZES. In the ZES, livestock farms exceeding a certain production of N as animal manure (between 12,500 and 20,000 kg per year, depending on the district) must develop a plan for the disposal of the excess N to reduce its environmental impact by either treating the slurry or transferring it outside the ZES for its application to crop land (MIRE, 2004).

In the Southeast of Bretagne, a group of pig farmers in the La Guerche de Bretagne and Argentré du Plessis cantons, producing 41 tons of excess N in the form of slurry, have developed a collective transfer and spreading plan. In the transfer plan, almost 7000 m³ of slurry would be transported (over 40 km) and injected in substi- tution of mineral fertiliser in crop land, belonging to farmers in the Bain de Bretagne and Le Grand Fougeray cantons. The group of farmers has chosen the transfer plan based on financial consider- ations, as the spreading area is at a relatively short distance and the transport units and application machinery are already available. The collective slurry transfer plan is now undergoing public review and inhabitants of the districts destined to receive slurry, govern- ment officials, agricultural advisers, environmental groups and the participating farmers are holding meetings to discuss their points of view with respect to the collective slurry management plan. Common questions in these meetings are: Is slurry transfer the best option in environmental terms? Why do the pig farmers not treat their slurry in their own cantons? What are the environmental advantages and disadvantages of such a slurry transfer plan?

Several studies have focused on specific aspects of slurry management, especially in relation to its fertiliser value and the emission of gaseous compounds during either storage (e.g. Portejoie et al., 2003; Berg et al., 2006; Park et al., 2006), treatment (e.g. Beline et al., 1999; Beline and Martinez, 2002; Melse and Verdoes, 2005; Loyon et al., 2007) or application to crop fields (e.g. Sandars et al., 2003; Rodhe and Etana, 2005; Sanchez and Gonzalez, 2005). Each of these studies measures or calculates gaseous emis- sions for specific types of slurry (e.g. from cattle or pigs, with different diets and at different physiological stages), under specific bioclimatic conditions (e.g. temperature, soils) and with specific techniques for storage, treatment or application.

Studies of the overall environmental performance of different slurry management alternatives seem, however, to be lacking. Such studies would be of great importance to fully understand the advantages and disadvantages of contrasting options for slurry management. Moreover, the study of collective forms of slurry management has not been dealt with in the scientific literature. Collective slurry transfer plans might represent the most viable form of managing excess slurry when farmers (a) do not produce enough excess slurry to install a financially viable individual treatment station, (b) are situated close to cantons not classified as ZES where they can apply their excess slurry to crop land.

The objective of this study is to comprehensively compare the environmental performance of two collective slurry management strategies (i.e. transfer VS. treatment) and contribute to a trans- parent dialogue between the different stakeholders. The environ- mental evaluation is carried out by Life Cycle Assessment and the

comparison considers four impact categories: eutrophication, acidification, climate change, and non-renewable energy use. The scenarios under analysis and the quantification of their gaseous emissions and resource use are presented in Section 2. In Section 3 we present and discuss the results for the four impacts and in Section 4 we present the conclusions of the study and recapitulate some of the main challenges and opportunities for further envi- ronmental assessment of different collective slurry management strategies.

Studies of the overall environmental performance of different slurry management alternatives seem, however, to be lacking. Such studies would be of great importance to fully understand the advantages and disadvantages of contrasting options for slurry management. Moreover, the study of collective forms of slurry management has not been dealt with in the scientific literature. The environ- mental evaluation is carried out by Life Cycle Assessment and the

comparison considers four impact categories: eutrophication, acidification, climate change, and non-renewable energy use. The scenarios under analysis and the quantification of their gaseous emissions and resource use are presented in Section 2. In Section 3 we present and discuss the results for the four impacts and in Section 4 we present the conclusions of the study and recapitulate some of the main challenges and opportunities for further envi- ronmental assessment of different collective slurry management strategies.

9: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a tool for environmental evalu- ation that has attracted much attention in the last decade, as it allows the comparison of different products or activities, based on the quantification of their potential environmental impacts related to the emissions of pollutants to soil, air and water as well as the consumption of resources throughout their life cycle (Guinée et al., 2002).

The goal of this study is to compare the environmental impacts of two contrasting collective slurry management scenarios: (1) A slurry transfer scenario, in which excess slurry is transported and applied to crop land in substitution of mineral fertilisers and (2) a slurry treatment scenario in which excess slurry is treated in a collective biological treatment station located within the ZES. The scope of the study is limited to slurry management as slurry production is considered to be identical in the two scenarios. As the final output generated by the two management systems differs (i.e. for the transfer scenario it is 1 m³ of raw slurry applied to arable land and, for the treatment scenario, it is the composted solid fraction, resulting from the treatment process, applied to arable land), the chosen functional unit or unit of analysis is 1 m³ of raw slurry being either transferred or treated.

11: <header>2.1. The slurry and its management scenarios </header>

The slurry to be transferred or treated originates from finishing pigs, as this slurry has the highest concentration of nutrients (Levasseur, 2005). Characteristics of the slurry produced by fin- ishing pigs are presented in Table 1.

The collective slurry transfer and spreading plan has been developed by a group of pig farmers. They have contacted crop- producing farmers willing to receive slurry, agreed on a spreading calendar in relation to crops grown, and settled contracts with enterprises and co-operatives for the slurry transport and injection. Data used for the analysis of this scenario was provided by the group of farmers and the cooperative in charge of slurry spreading. The treatment scenario was included in the analysis by the authors, in order to conduct a comparative analysis and explore other options for slurry management. Data used for the analysis of this scenario came from studies made in treatment stations in the region and enterprises specialised in slurry treatment. A schematic representation of the two scenarios is presented in Fig. 1.

The transfer scenario includes: on-farm slurry storage, its transport to the spreading area, its intermediate storage, and its injection into crop land. On farm, slurry is stored in circular uncovered tanks of reinforced concrete. To calculate the average level of slurry in the storage tanks and the residence time of 1 m³ of slurry, we have considered that the capacity of the tank corre- sponds to 8 months of slurry production (as stipulated by law in the case of spreading), that the production of slurry is continuous throughout the year and that the outflow from the tank is dictated by the spreading calendar of crops. Main crops include cereals (wheat, maize, oats), receiving slurry between February and April; rapeseed, receiving slurry in September; and grassland, receiving slurry all year round. The distribution of slurry spread during the year is as follows: 25% of the annual production in February, 34% in March, 17% in April, 8% in June and 16% in September. Average distance between the pig farmers and the area receiving the slurry is 39.2 km and the transport is done with a semi-trailer truck equipped with a 25 m³ payload. Once in the spreading area, the slurry is temporarily stored in a flexible tank (200 of PVC coated polyester (WINBAGTM) and then applied to the soil with a TerragatorT 2104. For both arable crops and grassland, applica- tion of slurry is done by shallow injection.

For the treatment scenario, a biological treatment procedure has been chosen. In Bretagne there are about 400 treatment stations of which 77% are of the biological type, accounting for 88% of the slurry treated. Other treatment methods in the region include physicochemical procedures and composting, the later accounting for 7% of the total volume of slurry treated (Levasseur and Lemaire, 2006). Biogas production from pig slurry in Bretagne is still in an experimental phase and, at present, there is not a single working station. This is partly due to the fact that such type of slurry treatment, although producing energy, does not solve the problem of excess nutrients, as the digested sludge contains as many nutrients as the input slurry.

The treatment scenario consist of five processes: on-farm slurry storage, its transport to the collective treatment plant, the treat- ment itself, the transport of compost (a by-product of the slurry treatment process), and the application of compost to crop land. On-farm, slurry is stored in circular uncovered tanks of reinforced concrete. To calculate the average level of slurry in the storage tanks and the residence time of 1 m³ of slurry, we have considered that the capacity of the tank corresponds to 6 months of slurry production (as stipulated by law in the case of a collective treat- ment plant), that the production of slurry is continuous throughout the year and that it is delivered three times a year to the collective treatment plant. Average distance between the pig farmers and the treatment plant is 12.1 km and the transport is done with a semi- trailer truck equipped with a 25 m³ payload. The slurry treatment is of the so-called aerobic or biological type (nitrification/denitrifi- cation), with previous separation of the solid and liquid fractions of the slurry with a centrifuge and re-circulation of sludge (Fig. 2 and an average abatement efficiency of 70% of the total N and 90% of the

ammoniacal N (Loyon et al., 2004). After the separation of the solid and liquid fractions, the liquid fraction is submitted to an inter- mittent aeration sustaining a bacterial process in which nitrogen is first oxidised into nitrates (nitrification) and then nitrates are reduced to molecular nitrogen (denitrification), harmlessly released to the atmosphere. After treatment, the liquid fraction is left to decant, the sludge is recirculated and the supernatant is stored in a lagoon for its occasional use irrigating the lawn and garden around the station. The solid fraction is composted for 9 weeks, involving the addition of 3% of straw and mechanical turning (Le Bris et al., 2005). Compost produced in the region is commonly transported to a cereal production region at 200 km distance for its utilisation in substitution of fertilisers.

Fig. 1. Schematic presentation of the processes involved in the two slurry management scenarios. Shaded boxes are not included in the study.

The transfer scenario includes: on-farm slurry storage, its transport to the spreading area, its intermediate storage, and its injection into crop land. On farm, slurry is stored in circular uncovered tanks of reinforced concrete. To calculate the average level of slurry in the storage tanks and the residence time of 1 m³ of slurry, we have considered that the capacity of the tank corre- sponds to 8 months of slurry production (as stipulated by law in the case of spreading), that the production of slurry is continuous throughout the year and that the outflow from the tank is dictated by the spreading calendar of crops. Main crops include cereals (wheat, maize, oats), receiving slurry between February and April; rapeseed, receiving slurry in September; and grassland, receiving slurry all year round. The distribution of slurry spread during the year is as follows: 25% of the annual production in February, 34% in March, 17% in April, 8% in June and 16% in September. Average distance between the pig farmers and the area receiving the slurry is 39.2 km and the transport is done with a semi-trailer truck equipped with a 25 m³ payload. Once in the spreading area, the slurry is temporarily stored in a flexible tank (200 of PVC coated polyester (WINBAGTM) and then applied to the soil with a TerragatorT 2104. For both arable crops and grassland, applica- tion of slurry is done by shallow injection.

For the treatment scenario, a biological treatment procedure has been chosen. In Bretagne there are about 400 treatment stations of which 77% are of the biological type, accounting for 88% of the slurry treated. Other treatment methods in the region include physicochemical procedures and composting, the later accounting for 7% of the total volume of slurry treated (Levasseur and Lemaire, 2006). Biogas production from pig slurry in Bretagne is still in an experimental phase and, at present, there is not a single working station. This is partly due to the fact that such type of slurry treatment, although producing energy, does not solve the problem of excess nutrients, as the digested sludge contains as many nutrients as the input slurry.

The treatment scenario consist of five processes: on-farm slurry storage, its transport to the collective treatment plant, the treat- ment itself, the transport of compost (a by-product of the slurry treatment process), and the application of compost to crop land. On-farm, slurry is stored in circular uncovered tanks of reinforced concrete. To calculate the average level of slurry in the storage tanks and the residence time of 1 m³ of slurry, we have considered that the capacity of the tank corresponds to 6 months of slurry production (as stipulated by law in the case of a collective treat- ment plant), that the production of slurry is continuous throughout the year and that it is delivered three times a year to the collective treatment plant. Average distance between the pig farmers and the treatment plant is 12.1 km and the transport is done with a semi- trailer truck equipped with a 25 m³ payload. The slurry treatment is of the so-called aerobic or biological type (nitrification/denitrifi- cation), with previous separation of the solid and liquid fractions of the slurry with a centrifuge and re-circulation of sludge (Fig. 2 and an average abatement efficiency of 70% of the total N and 90% of the

ammoniacal N (Loyon et al., 2004). After the separation of the solid and liquid fractions, the liquid fraction is submitted to an inter- mittent aeration sustaining a bacterial process in which nitrogen is first oxidised into nitrates (nitrification) and then nitrates are reduced to molecular nitrogen (denitrification), harmlessly released to the atmosphere. After treatment, the liquid fraction is left to decant, the sludge is recirculated and the supernatant is stored in a lagoon for its occasional use irrigating the lawn and garden around the station. The solid fraction is composted for 9 weeks, involving the addition of 3% of straw and mechanical turning (Le Bris et al., 2005). Compost produced in the region is commonly transported to a cereal production region at 200 km distance for its utilisation in substitution of fertilisers.

17: Most important gaseous emissions during storage, treatment and application of slurry and compost are ammonia (NH3), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). Emissions of CO2 from the slurry storage, treatment or during composting, are not taken into account, as this is considered part of the so called short carbon cycle, i.e. resulting from CO2 taken up recently by crops. However, emission of CO2 which originates from fossil energy use is taken into account.

For both scenarios, NH3, N20 and CH4 emissions during storage and treatment are based on emission factors measured along the different stages of a biological treatment station in Bretagne with a floating chamber and determined by infrared detection (CH4, N20) and gas chromatography (NH3), reported in Loyon et al. (2005, 2007). Because N concentration in the slurry used in our study (i.e. from finishing pigs only -Table 1) is higher than that in the slurry used by Loyon et al. (2005, 2007) (i.e. mixed slurry), emission factors for NH3 and N20 were proportionally corrected in relation to the ammoniacal and total N content of the slurry, respectively. A mass and nutrient balance was computed to calculate total gaseous losses at different stages of both slurry management scenarios and to know the characteristics of the resulting products. Table 2 shows the characteristics of the slurry and its liquid derivatives in terms of ammoniacal and total N content in the different stages of the treatment process, their emission coefficients for NH3, N20 and CH4 as well as the quantity with respect to 1 m³ of slurry entering the treatment process, the air-contact surface area for 1 m³ of slurry entering the treatment process and the residence time. The quan- tity of slurry and its liquid derivatives (resulting from the separa- tion of slurry at the centrifuge and after decantation) and their residence time were based on Loyon et al. (2005, 2007) and the surface area was calculated in relation to tank and lagoon sizes. Nitrogen losses during composting of the solid fraction, essentially in the form of NH3, the quantity (123 kg of compost m 3 of slurry) and characteristics of the final product were based on Le Bris et al. (2005), CH4 emissions during composting were based on Loyon et al. (2005, 2007). NH3 emissions from slurry and compost
Fig. 2. Schematic presentation of the slurry treatment process.

application are based on reference emission factors. For slurry application, 20% of the ammoniacal N is considered to be lost in the form of NH3 (Morvan and Leterme, 2001) but these losses are considered to be reduced by 80% due to its injection rather than its surface spreading (UNECE, 1999; Basset-Mens et al., 2007), result- ing in 4% of the slurry ammoniacal N emitted as NH3. For the compost, 50% of the ammoniacal N is considered to be lost in the form of NH3 (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). For N20 emissions, refer- ence values from IPCC (2006) are used, representing 1% and 2% of the total nitrogen of compost and slurry, respectively.

Non-renewable energy use in the transfer scenario includes the energy used for the transport of slurry to the spreading area (2.49 ) of diesel and for its injection (0.8 of diesel m (Anonymous, 2005). For the treatment scenario it includes the diesel used for the transportation of slurry to the treatment plant (0.76 1 of diesel m-3, the electricity used for the treatment (centrifuge, aeration, pump- ing) (18.7 kWh m-3 (Levasseur et al., 2003), the transport of compost to the application area (1.51 ) of diesel per 123 kg of compost produced by 1 m³ of raw slurry) and the spreading of compost (0.0791 of diesel per 123 kg produced by 1 m³ of raw slurry) (van der Werf, unpublished data). The scenarios include resource use and emissions of pollutants associated with the production of the concrete and plastics (PVC, PET) needed for the storage and treatment of slurry, as well as those associated with the machinery needed for the injection of slurry (in the case of transfer) and the spreading of compost (in the case of treatment). Emissions related to the extraction of raw materials and transport are based on BUWAL 250 (BUWAL, 1996). Because of its very minor influence, energy used for the production of trucks for transport was not included in this study.

Both the slurry in the transfer scenario and the compost produced in the treatment scenario, are applied to crop land in substitution of chemical fertilisers. For a complete environmental evaluation it is therefore necessary to include (i.e. subtract) the impacts related to the production, transport and application of these saved fertilisers. One m³ of injected slurry supplies, after losses during storage and application, 5.21 kg N, 3.31 kg of P2O5 and 4.96 kg of K20; Mineral Fertiliser Equivalents (MFEs) for these nutrients are 65%, 95% and 100%, respectively. The compost produced by 1 m³ of raw slurry (123 kg) supplies 1.65 kg of N, 3.09 kg of P2O5 and .57 kg of K2O; MFEs for these nutrients in compost are 10%, 88% and 100%, respectively. Because it does not substitute any fertiliser, we didn't consider the fertiliser savings for the supernatant. MFEs were calculated in relation to slurry and compost characteristics and based on studies carried out within the region of study (Morvan and Leterme, 2001; Morvan et al., 2005; Linères et al., 2005). Resource use and emissions associated with the production, transportation and application of the chemical fertilisers are based on Davis and Haglund (1999) and van der Werf (unpublished data), emissions associated with the application of such fertilisers are considered to be 2% for NH3 (ECETOC, 1994) and 1% for N20 (IPCC, 2006).

(2005, 2007) and the surface area was calculated in relation to tank and lagoon sizes. Nitrogen losses during composting of the solid fraction, essentially in the form of NH3, the quantity (123 kg of compost m 3 of slurry) and characteristics of the final product were based on Le Bris et al. (2005), CH4 emissions during composting were based on Loyon et al. (2005, 2007). NH3 emissions from slurry and compost

application are based on reference emission factors. For slurry application, 20% of the ammoniacal N is considered to be lost in the form of NH3 (Morvan and Leterme, 2001) but these losses are considered to be reduced by 80% due to its injection rather than its surface spreading (UNECE, 1999; Basset-Mens et al., 2007), result- ing in 4% of the slurry ammoniacal N emitted as NH3. For the compost, 50% of the ammoniacal N is considered to be lost in the form of NH3 (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). For N20 emissions, refer- ence values from IPCC (2006) are used, representing 1% and 2% of the total nitrogen of compost and slurry, respectively.

Non-renewable energy use in the transfer scenario includes the energy used for the transport of slurry to the spreading area (2.49 ) of diesel and for its injection (0.8 of diesel m (Anonymous, 2005). For the treatment scenario it includes the diesel used for the transportation of slurry to the treatment plant (0.76 1 of diesel m-3, the electricity used for the treatment (centrifuge, aeration, pump- ing) (18.7 kWh m-3 (Levasseur et al., 2003), the transport of compost to the application area (1.51 ) of diesel per 123 kg of compost produced by 1 m³ of raw slurry) and the spreading of compost (0.0791 of diesel per 123 kg produced by 1 m³ of raw slurry) (van der Werf, unpublished data). The scenarios include resource use and emissions of pollutants associated with the production of the concrete and plastics (PVC, PET) needed for the storage and treatment of slurry, as well as those associated with the machinery needed for the injection of slurry (in the case of transfer) and the spreading of compost (in the case of treatment). Emissions related to the extraction of raw materials and transport are based on BUWAL 250 (BUWAL, 1996). Because of its very minor influence, energy used for the production of trucks for transport was not included in this study.

Both the slurry in the transfer scenario and the compost produced in the treatment scenario, are applied to crop land in substitution of chemical fertilisers. For a complete environmental evaluation it is therefore necessary to include (i.e. subtract) the impacts related to the production, transport and application of these saved fertilisers. One m³ of injected slurry supplies, after losses during storage and application, 5.21 kg N, 3.31 kg of P2O5 and 4.96 kg of K20; Mineral Fertiliser Equivalents (MFEs) for these nutrients are 65%, 95% and 100%, respectively. The compost produced by 1 m³ of raw slurry (123 kg) supplies 1.65 kg of N, 3.09 kg of P2O5 and .57 kg of K2O; MFEs for these nutrients in compost are 10%, 88% and 100%, respectively. Because it does not substitute any fertiliser, we didn't consider the fertiliser savings for the supernatant. MFEs were calculated in relation to slurry and compost characteristics and based on studies carried out within the region of study (Morvan and Leterme, 2001; Morvan et al., 2005; Linères et al., 2005). Resource use and emissions associated with the production, transportation and application of the chemical fertilisers are based on Davis and Haglund (1999) and van der Werf (unpublished data), emissions associated with the application of such fertilisers are considered to be 2% for NH3 (ECETOC, 1994) and 1% for N20 (IPCC, 2006).

Total (direct and indirect) emission and resource use are aggregated and expressed in terms of three impact categories (Guinée et al., 2002): eutrophication (in kg -eq.), acidification and climate change (in kg CO2-eq.). Energy use is expressed in terms of non-renewable energy use (in MJ of Low Heating Value (LHV)-eq.).

Such potential environmental impacts are calculated from resource use and emissions of individual substances, which are multiplied by a characterisation factor for each impact category to which it may potentially contribute (Heijungs et al., 1992). Char- acterisation factors are substance-specific, quantitative represen- tations of the additional environmental pressure per unit emission of a substance (Huijbregts et al., 2000). The characterisation factors used in this study are given below for each impact category.

Eutrophication covers all potential impacts of high environ- mental levels of macronutrients, in particular N and P. As recom- mended by Guinée et al. (2002), eutrophication potential was calculated using the generic factors in kg PO4-equivalents: NH3: 0.35, NO3: 0.1, NO2: 0.13, NOx: 0.13, PO4: 1. Acidifying pollutants have a wide variety of impacts on soil, groundwater, surface waters, biological organisms, ecosystems and materials (buildings). As recommended by Guinée et al. (2002), acidification potential was calculated using the average European factors by Huijbregts (1999) in kg SO2-equivalents, NH3: 1.6, NO2: 0.5, NOx: 0.5, SO2: 1.2. Climate change was defined here as the impact of emissions on the heat radiation absorption of the atmosphere. As recommended by Guinée et al. (2002), Global Warming Potential for a 100-year time horizon (GWP100) was calculated according to the GWP factors by IPCC (IPCC, 1997) in kg CO2-equivalents, CO2: 1, N20: 310, CH4: 21. Finally, non-renewable energy use refers to the depletion of energetic resources. Non-renewable energy use was calculated using the Lower Heating Values (LHV) proposed in SimaPro V. 6 for: crude oil gas from oil production MJ natural gas (35 MJ m-3), uranium (451000 MJ kg 1), coal and lignite (PRé Consultants, 2001).

26: 3.1. The emissions and resource use of the slurry management scenarios

Table 3 shows the direct material and energetic resource use as well as direct gaseous emissions for the two scenarios. Avoided fertiliser use and avoided direct gaseous emissions are included in the comparison, in order to take into account the advantages of using slurry or compost in substitution of fertilisers.

Although the on-farm storage tanks of the treatment scenario are smaller than those in the transfer scenario, the former scenario consumes more material resources, as the treatment station requires extra concrete and plastics. The transfer scenario consumes more diesel for transport of slurry than does the treat- ment scenario for the transport of slurry and of compost. However, the centrifugation of slurry and the aeration process of the treat- ment station is carried out with electric power, increasing the overall energetic consumption of the treatment scenario.

Emissions of CH4 are similar for the two scenarios. In the transfer scenario, CH4 is entirely emitted during the on-farm storage of slurry whereas, in the treatment scenario, 54% of the CH4 is emitted during on-farm storage and 47% during slurry treatment. During treatment, 62% of the CH4 is emitted during the pre-treat- ment storage and homogenisation, 33% during composting of the solid fraction, and 5% from the other stages of the treatment process.

Emissions of NH3 for the treatment scenario are nearly the double of those in the transfer scenario. In the transfer scenario, 89% of the NH3 losses take place during the on-farm storage and the rest during and following injection of slurry. In the treatment scenario, most important NH3 losses, 55%, occur during the treat- ment process itself, of which 75% is emitted while composting the solid fraction, 16% during the pre-treatment and homogenisation phase and the rest during decantation and the storage of super- natant. On-farm storage of slurry and compost application account for 45% of the ammonia emissions in the treatment scenario.

Emissions of N20 are higher for the transfer scenario than for the treatment scenario. Slurry application is responsible for most N20 emissions in the transfer scenario. Most N20 emissions in the treatment scenario occur as a result of the application of compost to crop fields; during treatment of slurry only small quantities of N20 are emitted as a result of the intermittent aeration in the biological treatment.

When applied to crop land, slurry and compost represent a source of nutrients allowing a reduction in the use of chemical fertilisers and a reduction of gaseous emissions incurred during application of such fertilisers. Slurry injected in the transfer scenario represents a more important fertiliser savings than the application of compost produced by the treatment scenario, as nutrient availability for crops is higher from slurry than from compost and, during the treatment of slurry, important quantities of N are released to the atmosphere in the form of N2.

29: <header>3.2. The environmental impact of the slurry management scenarios </header>

Fig. 3 compares the two scenarios for the four impacts nor- malised in relation to the values of the treatment scenario. The overall environmental performance of the transfer scenario is better than that of the treatment scenario. The difference between the two scenarios is especially important for eutrophication and acidification as well as for non-renewable energy use.

For better understanding of the processes responsible for the impacts, Fig. 4 shows the contribution of each phase along the transfer and treatment scenarios to the impacts and Table 4 shows the contribution of different substances, directly and indirectly emitted along the different phases of the two scenarios.

NH3 is the most important contributor to eutrophication and acidification for the two scenarios (Table 4). In the transfer scenario, on-farm storage is the main phase responsible for these impacts, while in the treatment scenario it is the biological treatment phase, specifically during the storage/homogenisation and composting processes.

In the transfer scenario, the impacts of slurry injection for eutrophication and acidification are of similar magnitude as those avoided by the substitution of fertilisers, of the latter about 50% is due to NH3 emissions during their application and the rest related to other substances emitted during the production and trans- portation of fertilisers (e.g. NO2, P and SO2) (Fig. 4, Table 4). On the contrary, in the treatment scenario, the substitution of fertilisers only compensates for ca. 30% of the impacts incurred during compost spreading due its low N content and low MFE value (Fig. 4).

For climate change, CH4 is the major contributor for the two scenarios (Table 4). As for the previous impacts, in the transfer scenario, on-farm storage contributes most; while in the treatment scenario, the storage and treatment phases contribute to the same extent. CO2 is mainly emitted during transport of slurry and compost and N20 is mainly emitted during and after the applica- tion of slurry, for the transfer scenario, and of compost, for the treatment scenario.

In the transfer scenario, the impacts of slurry injection for eutrophication and acidification are of similar magnitude as those avoided by the substitution of fertilisers, of the latter about 50% is due to NH3 emissions during their application and the rest related to other substances emitted during the production and trans- portation of fertilisers (e.g. NO2, P and SO2) (Fig. 4, Table 4). On the contrary, in the treatment scenario, the substitution of fertilisers only compensates for ca. 30% of the impacts incurred during compost spreading due its low N content and low MFE value (Fig. 4).

For climate change, CH4 is the major contributor for the two scenarios (Table 4). As for the previous impacts, in the transfer scenario, on-farm storage contributes most; while in the treatment scenario, the storage and treatment phases contribute to the same extent. CO2 is mainly emitted during transport of slurry and compost and N20 is mainly emitted during and after the applica- tion of slurry, for the transfer scenario, and of compost, for the treatment scenario.

3: In the environmental evaluation of these two contrasting scenarios for collective excess slurry management, the transfer of slurry represents a better option. However, both the environmental performance of the scenarios and the method we used for the environmental evaluation of the scenarios can be improved.
Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of two slurry management scenarios expressed as percentage of impacts for the treatment scenario.

Covering storage tanks can reduce NH3 emissions and conse- quently eutrophication and acidification. Portejoie et al. (2003), studying different cover materials, have observed a 74% to 100% decrease of NH3 losses during storage. However, Berg et al. (2006) have shown that, while NH3 emission might be reduced by covering storage tanks, emissions of CH4 and N20 might be increased, which would increase climate change. Also, different techniques of slurry application will also have an effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the transfer scenario. In the transfer scenario, we have used the IPCC (2006) N20 emission factor for liquid manures (i.e. 2% of the total N), however, injection of slurry, relative to surface application, might increase N20 emissions as pores in the soil are filled with slurry and so-called anaerobic hot spots might be created increasing denitrification rates (Dendooven et al., 1998). However, given the lack of sufficient published results quantifying this effect we did not take it into account in this study.

Key assumptions might have an influence on the environmental performance of the collective slurry management scenarios eval- uated here. In both the transfer and treatment scenarios, we have assumed best technical and organisational practices, and standard coefficients from scientific literature were used to calculate emis- sions and efficiencies. For example, we have assumed that injection of slurry reduces ammonia emissions by 80%, while in reality this abatement coefficient has great variability depending on, amongst others, depth of injection and dose of application, chemical char- acteristics of the slurry, soil characteristics as well as meteorolog- ical conditions at the moment of injection (cf. Misselbrook et al., 2002). Equally, for the treatment scenario we have assumed that the biological treatment plant has 90% and 70% treatment efficiency for TAN and TKN, respectively. In reality such efficiencies, and their associated N20 emissions, are only reached when the aerobic to anoxic ratio in the intermittent aeration is well calibrated in rela- tion to the slurry chemical characteristics (cf. Beline and Martinez, 2002; Beline et al., 1999) and it might show variations in relation to temperature as it is driven by a biological process.

Also, in relation to the MFEs, we have assumed that part of the nutrients coming from either raw slurry or compost (but also K in the supernatant) do not substitute any synthetic fertiliser use because it is either immobilised or fixed in the soil (or, simply not conceived as a form of fertiliser). In the long term, however, such nutrients will become available for crop growth and therefore MFE values to be used for slurry and compost will increase over time with continued use of slurry and compost. Anyway, given the loss of
Fig. 4. Contribution of the different stages of the transfer and treatment scenarios to four environmental impacts expressed per m³ of slurry transferred or treated.

N in its molecular form during the treatment of slurry, the use of raw slurry in substitution of fertilisers will always present less impact than treatment of slurry, even when considering a 100% MFE for all nutrients (N, P and K) present in either the raw slurry, compost or supernatant, that are applied to land.

In relation to gaseous emissions, we have used coefficients from one of the most complete studies related to gaseous losses in slurry management in France, based on measures along the different stages of the storage and treatment processes (Loyon et al., 2005, 2007). However, the computation of precise gaseous losses in slurry management faces major technical difficulties, their continuous measurement is expensive and the results are difficult to extrapo- late to different regions, seasons and slurry compositions. For better estimation of gaseous emissions, it would be important to develop process based algorithms that take into account slurry
characteristics and meteorological conditions (e.g. Sommer et al., 2004, 2006).

Another important factor affecting gaseous emissions is the residence time and contact surface area between the slurry and the atmosphere at different stages along the slurry management process. Guerrin (2001, 2004) and Guerrin and Paillat (2003) have developed dynamic simulation models to evaluate the operational feasibility of different slurry management scenarios. The models calculate, taking into account the resources available for transport/ spreading and the climatic conditions, the replenishment level of slurry storage tanks in relation to the production of slurry, the size of the storage tanks as well as the calendar and technique of slurry application to crop land or delivery to treatment stations. In future studies it would be important to apply such models to use more precise estimates of the slurry-atmosphere contact area to calculate

In relation to the energy costs of slurry management, future studies might include the use of different means of transport of the slurry and the treatment's by-products, as well as the production and use of biogas from methane digesters. However, in relation to biogas production, it is important to notice that pig slurry is one of the least productive substrates, due to its low dry matter content and therefore supplementary sources of carbon (e.g. straw, cattle manure) must be added to increase methane production. In addi- tion, the by-product resulting from the anaerobic digestion (digestate) will contain the same amount of nutrients as the raw slurry and its nitrogen it will be mainly in ammoniacal form, much more prone to losses through volatilisation (Sommer, 1997).

In relation to the energy costs of slurry management, future studies might include the use of different means of transport of the slurry and the treatment's by-products, as well as the production and use of biogas from methane digesters. However, in relation to biogas production, it is important to notice that pig slurry is one of the least productive substrates, due to its low dry matter content and therefore supplementary sources of carbon (e.g. straw, cattle manure) must be added to increase methane production. In addi- tion, the by-product resulting from the anaerobic digestion (digestate) will contain the same amount of nutrients as the raw slurry and its nitrogen it will be mainly in ammoniacal form, much more prone to losses through volatilisation (Sommer, 1997).

Covering storage tanks can reduce NH3 emissions and conse- quently eutrophication and acidification. Portejoie et al. (2003), studying different cover materials, have observed a 74% to 100% decrease of NH3 losses during storage. However, Berg et al. (2006) have shown that, while NH3 emission might be reduced by covering storage tanks, emissions of CH4 and N20 might be increased, which would increase climate change. Also, different techniques of slurry application will also have an effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the transfer scenario. In the transfer scenario, we have used the IPCC (2006) N20 emission factor for liquid manures (i.e. 2% of the total N), however, injection of slurry, relative to surface application, might increase N20 emissions as pores in the soil are filled with slurry and so-called anaerobic hot spots might be created increasing denitrification rates (Dendooven et al., 1998). However, given the lack of sufficient published results quantifying this effect we did not take it into account in this study.

Key assumptions might have an influence on the environmental performance of the collective slurry management scenarios eval- uated here. In both the transfer and treatment scenarios, we have assumed best technical and organisational practices, and standard coefficients from scientific literature were used to calculate emis- sions and efficiencies. For example, we have assumed that injection of slurry reduces ammonia emissions by 80%, while in reality this abatement coefficient has great variability depending on, amongst others, depth of injection and dose of application, chemical char- acteristics of the slurry, soil characteristics as well as meteorolog- ical conditions at the moment of injection (cf. Misselbrook et al., 2002). Equally, for the treatment scenario we have assumed that the biological treatment plant has 90% and 70% treatment efficiency for TAN and TKN, respectively. In reality such efficiencies, and their associated N20 emissions, are only reached when the aerobic to anoxic ratio in the intermittent aeration is well calibrated in rela- tion to the slurry chemical characteristics (cf. Beline and Martinez, 2002; Beline et al., 1999) and it might show variations in relation to temperature as it is driven by a biological process.

Also, in relation to the MFEs, we have assumed that part of the nutrients coming from either raw slurry or compost (but also K in the supernatant) do not substitute any synthetic fertiliser use because it is either immobilised or fixed in the soil (or, simply not conceived as a form of fertiliser). In the long term, however, such nutrients will become available for crop growth and therefore MFE values to be used for slurry and compost will increase over time with continued use of slurry and compost. In relation to the energy costs of slurry management, future studies might include the use of different means of transport of the slurry and the treatment's by-products, as well as the production and use of biogas from methane digesters. However, in relation to biogas production, it is important to notice that pig slurry is one of the least productive substrates, due to its low dry matter content and therefore supplementary sources of carbon (e.g. straw, cattle manure) must be added to increase methane production. In addi- tion, the by-product resulting from the anaerobic digestion (digestate) will contain the same amount of nutrients as the raw slurry and its nitrogen it will be mainly in ammoniacal form, much more prone to losses through volatilisation (Sommer, 1997).
9: In the environmental evaluation of these two contrasting scenarios for collective excess slurry management, the transfer of slurry represents a better option. However, both the environmental performance of the scenarios and the method we used for the environmental evaluation of the scenarios can be improved.

Covering storage tanks can reduce NH3 emissions and conse- quently eutrophication and acidification. Portejoie et al. (2003), studying different cover materials, have observed a 74% to 100% decrease of NH3 losses during storage. However, Berg et al. (2006) have shown that, while NH3 emission might be reduced by covering storage tanks, emissions of CH4 and N20 might be increased, which would increase climate change. Also, different techniques of slurry application will also have an effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the transfer scenario. In the transfer scenario, we have used the IPCC (2006) N20 emission factor for liquid manures (i.e. 2% of the total N), however, injection of slurry, relative to surface application, might increase N20 emissions as pores in the soil are filled with slurry and so-called anaerobic hot spots might be created increasing denitrification rates (Dendooven et al., 1998). However, given the lack of sufficient published results quantifying this effect we did not take it into account in this study.

Key assumptions might have an influence on the environmental performance of the collective slurry management scenarios eval- uated here. In both the transfer and treatment scenarios, we have assumed best technical and organisational practices, and standard coefficients from scientific literature were used to calculate emis- sions and efficiencies. For example, we have assumed that injection of slurry reduces ammonia emissions by 80%, while in reality this abatement coefficient has great variability depending on, amongst others, depth of injection and dose of application, chemical char- acteristics of the slurry, soil characteristics as well as meteorolog- ical conditions at the moment of injection (cf. Misselbrook et al., 2002). Equally, for the treatment scenario we have assumed that the biological treatment plant has 90% and 70% treatment efficiency for TAN and TKN, respectively. In reality such efficiencies, and their associated N20 emissions, are only reached when the aerobic to anoxic ratio in the intermittent aeration is well calibrated in rela- tion to the slurry chemical characteristics (cf. Beline and Martinez, 2002; Beline et al., 1999) and it might show variations in relation to temperature as it is driven by a biological process.

Also, in relation to the MFEs, we have assumed that part of the nutrients coming from either raw slurry or compost (but also K in the supernatant) do not substitute any synthetic fertiliser use because it is either immobilised or fixed in the soil (or, simply not conceived as a form of fertiliser). In the long term, however, such nutrients will become available for crop growth and therefore MFE values to be used for slurry and compost will increase over time with continued use of slurry and compost. Anyway, given the loss of
Fig. 4. Contribution of the different stages of the transfer and treatment scenarios to four environmental impacts expressed per m³ of slurry transferred or treated.

N in its molecular form during the treatment of slurry, the use of raw slurry in substitution of fertilisers will always present less impact than treatment of slurry, even when considering a 100% MFE for all nutrients (N, P and K) present in either the raw slurry, compost or supernatant, that are applied to land.

In relation to gaseous emissions, we have used coefficients from one of the most complete studies related to gaseous losses in slurry management in France, based on measures along the different stages of the storage and treatment processes (Loyon et al., 2005, 2007). However, the computation of precise gaseous losses in slurry management faces major technical difficulties, their continuous measurement is expensive and the results are difficult to extrapo- late to different regions, seasons and slurry compositions. For better estimation of gaseous emissions, it would be important to develop process based algorithms that take into account slurry
characteristics and meteorological conditions (e.g. Sommer et al., 2004, 2006).

Another important factor affecting gaseous emissions is the residence time and contact surface area between the slurry and the atmosphere at different stages along the slurry management process. Guerrin (2001, 2004) and Guerrin and Paillat (2003) have developed dynamic simulation models to evaluate the operational feasibility of different slurry management scenarios. The models calculate, taking into account the resources available for transport/ spreading and the climatic conditions, the replenishment level of slurry storage tanks in relation to the production of slurry, the size of the storage tanks as well as the calendar and technique of slurry application to crop land or delivery to treatment stations. In relation to the energy costs of slurry management, future studies might include the use of different means of transport of the slurry and the treatment's by-products, as well as the production and use of biogas from methane digesters. However, in relation to biogas production, it is important to notice that pig slurry is one of the least productive substrates, due to its low dry matter content and therefore supplementary sources of carbon (e.g. straw, cattle manure) must be added to increase methane production. In addi- tion, the by-product resulting from the anaerobic digestion (digestate) will contain the same amount of nutrients as the raw slurry and its nitrogen it will be mainly in ammoniacal form, much more prone to losses through volatilisation (Sommer, 1997).
12: In the environmental evaluation of these two contrasting scenarios for collective excess slurry management, the transfer of slurry represents a better option. However, both the environmental performance of the scenarios and the method we used for the environmental evaluation of the scenarios can be improved.
Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of two slurry management scenarios expressed as percentage of impacts for the treatment scenario.

Covering storage tanks can reduce NH3 emissions and conse- quently eutrophication and acidification. Portejoie et al. (2003), studying different cover materials, have observed a 74% to 100% decrease of NH3 losses during storage. However, Berg et al. (2006) have shown that, while NH3 emission might be reduced by covering storage tanks, emissions of CH4 and N20 might be increased, which would increase climate change. Also, different techniques of slurry application will also have an effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the transfer scenario. In the transfer scenario, we have used the IPCC (2006) N20 emission factor for liquid manures (i.e. 2% of the total N), however, injection of slurry, relative to surface application, might increase N20 emissions as pores in the soil are filled with slurry and so-called anaerobic hot spots might be created increasing denitrification rates (Dendooven et al., 1998). However, given the lack of sufficient published results quantifying this effect we did not take it into account in this study.

Key assumptions might have an influence on the environmental performance of the collective slurry management scenarios eval- uated here. In both the transfer and treatment scenarios, we have assumed best technical and organisational practices, and standard coefficients from scientific literature were used to calculate emis- sions and efficiencies. For example, we have assumed that injection of slurry reduces ammonia emissions by 80%, while in reality this abatement coefficient has great variability depending on, amongst others, depth of injection and dose of application, chemical char- acteristics of the slurry, soil characteristics as well as meteorolog- ical conditions at the moment of injection (cf. Misselbrook et al., 2002). Equally, for the treatment scenario we have assumed that the biological treatment plant has 90% and 70% treatment efficiency for TAN and TKN, respectively. In reality such efficiencies, and their associated N20 emissions, are only reached when the aerobic to anoxic ratio in the intermittent aeration is well calibrated in rela- tion to the slurry chemical characteristics (cf. Beline and Martinez, 2002; Beline et al., 1999) and it might show variations in relation to temperature as it is driven by a biological process.

Also, in relation to the MFEs, we have assumed that part of the nutrients coming from either raw slurry or compost (but also K in the supernatant) do not substitute any synthetic fertiliser use because it is either immobilised or fixed in the soil (or, simply not conceived as a form of fertiliser). In the long term, however, such nutrients will become available for crop growth and therefore MFE values to be used for slurry and compost will increase over time with continued use of slurry and compost. Anyway, given the loss of
Fig. 4. Contribution of the different stages of the transfer and treatment scenarios to four environmental impacts expressed per m³ of slurry transferred or treated.

N in its molecular form during the treatment of slurry, the use of raw slurry in substitution of fertilisers will always present less impact than treatment of slurry, even when considering a 100% MFE for all nutrients (N, P and K) present in either the raw slurry, compost or supernatant, that are applied to land.

In relation to gaseous emissions, we have used coefficients from one of the most complete studies related to gaseous losses in slurry management in France, based on measures along the different stages of the storage and treatment processes (Loyon et al., 2005, 2007). However, the computation of precise gaseous losses in slurry management faces major technical difficulties, their continuous measurement is expensive and the results are difficult to extrapo- late to different regions, seasons and slurry compositions. For better estimation of gaseous emissions, it would be important to develop process based algorithms that take into account slurry
characteristics and meteorological conditions (e.g. Sommer et al., 2004, 2006).

Another important factor affecting gaseous emissions is the residence time and contact surface area between the slurry and the atmosphere at different stages along the slurry management process. Guerrin (2001, 2004) and Guerrin and Paillat (2003) have developed dynamic simulation models to evaluate the operational feasibility of different slurry management scenarios. The models calculate, taking into account the resources available for transport/ spreading and the climatic conditions, the replenishment level of slurry storage tanks in relation to the production of slurry, the size of the storage tanks as well as the calendar and technique of slurry application to crop land or delivery to treatment stations. In relation to the energy costs of slurry management, future studies might include the use of different means of transport of the slurry and the treatment's by-products, as well as the production and use of biogas from methane digesters. However, in relation to biogas production, it is important to notice that pig slurry is one of the least productive substrates, due to its low dry matter content and therefore supplementary sources of carbon (e.g. straw, cattle manure) must be added to increase methane production. In addi- tion, the by-product resulting from the anaerobic digestion (digestate) will contain the same amount of nutrients as the raw slurry and its nitrogen it will be mainly in ammoniacal form, much more prone to losses through volatilisation (Sommer, 1997).
15: In the environmental evaluation of these two contrasting scenarios for collective excess slurry management, the transfer of slurry represents a better option. However, both the environmental performance of the scenarios and the method we used for the environmental evaluation of the scenarios can be improved.
Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of two slurry management scenarios expressed as percentage of impacts for the treatment scenario.

Covering storage tanks can reduce NH3 emissions and conse- quently eutrophication and acidification. Portejoie et al. (2003), studying different cover materials, have observed a 74% to 100% decrease of NH3 losses during storage. However, Berg et al. (2006) have shown that, while NH3 emission might be reduced by covering storage tanks, emissions of CH4 and N20 might be increased, which would increase climate change. Also, different techniques of slurry application will also have an effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the transfer scenario. In the transfer scenario, we have used the IPCC (2006) N20 emission factor for liquid manures (i.e. 2% of the total N), however, injection of slurry, relative to surface application, might increase N20 emissions as pores in the soil are filled with slurry and so-called anaerobic hot spots might be created increasing denitrification rates (Dendooven et al., 1998). However, given the lack of sufficient published results quantifying this effect we did not take it into account in this study.

Key assumptions might have an influence on the environmental performance of the collective slurry management scenarios eval- uated here. In both the transfer and treatment scenarios, we have assumed best technical and organisational practices, and standard coefficients from scientific literature were used to calculate emis- sions and efficiencies. For example, we have assumed that injection of slurry reduces ammonia emissions by 80%, while in reality this abatement coefficient has great variability depending on, amongst others, depth of injection and dose of application, chemical char- acteristics of the slurry, soil characteristics as well as meteorolog- ical conditions at the moment of injection (cf. Misselbrook et al., 2002). Equally, for the treatment scenario we have assumed that the biological treatment plant has 90% and 70% treatment efficiency for TAN and TKN, respectively. In reality such efficiencies, and their associated N20 emissions, are only reached when the aerobic to anoxic ratio in the intermittent aeration is well calibrated in rela- tion to the slurry chemical characteristics (cf. Beline and Martinez, 2002; Beline et al., 1999) and it might show variations in relation to temperature as it is driven by a biological process.

Also, in relation to the MFEs, we have assumed that part of the nutrients coming from either raw slurry or compost (but also K in the supernatant) do not substitute any synthetic fertiliser use because it is either immobilised or fixed in the soil (or, simply not conceived as a form of fertiliser). In the long term, however, such nutrients will become available for crop growth and therefore MFE values to be used for slurry and compost will increase over time with continued use of slurry and compost. Anyway, given the loss of

N in its molecular form during the treatment of slurry, the use of raw slurry in substitution of fertilisers will always present less impact than treatment of slurry, even when considering a 100% MFE for all nutrients (N, P and K) present in either the raw slurry, compost or supernatant, that are applied to land.

In relation to gaseous emissions, we have used coefficients from one of the most complete studies related to gaseous losses in slurry management in France, based on measures along the different stages of the storage and treatment processes (Loyon et al., 2005, 2007). However, the computation of precise gaseous losses in slurry management faces major technical difficulties, their continuous measurement is expensive and the results are difficult to extrapo- late to different regions, seasons and slurry compositions. For better estimation of gaseous emissions, it would be important to develop process based algorithms that take into account slurry
characteristics and meteorological conditions (e.g. Sommer et al., 2004, 2006).

Another important factor affecting gaseous emissions is the residence time and contact surface area between the slurry and the atmosphere at different stages along the slurry management process. Guerrin (2001, 2004) and Guerrin and Paillat (2003) have developed dynamic simulation models to evaluate the operational feasibility of different slurry management scenarios. The models calculate, taking into account the resources available for transport/ spreading and the climatic conditions, the replenishment level of slurry storage tanks in relation to the production of slurry, the size of the storage tanks as well as the calendar and technique of slurry application to crop land or delivery to treatment stations. 16: Excess slurry is an important environmental burden for inten- sive livestock production and, for the environmental evaluation of different management scenarios, a holistic approach is needed. In this study we used LCA, which allows the transparent multi-criteria environmental evaluation of different alternatives. It also pinpoints the main processes and substances responsible for different envi- ronmental impacts and can identify areas for improvement of the environmental performance of slurry management.

From this study we conclude that, for the two scenarios compared, the transfer of slurry and its injection in crop land in substitution of fertilisers poses less environmental threats than its biological treatment in a collective station, as the former produces less gaseous emissions and consumes less non-renewable energy. NH3 is the main substance contributing to the eutrophication and acidification associated with slurry management and it is mainly emitted during the storage of slurry, whether on-farm or in the treatment station. With respect to climate change, CH4 is the most important contributor and it is also mainly released during the storage of slurry. Concerning non-renewable energy use, the advantage of the transfer scenario resides in the fact that more nutrients are available for crop growth and therefore more fertil- isers are substituted as well as that important quantities of electric power are needed during the treatment process.

To refine the current analysis and due to the major influence of storage time and slurry/atmosphere contact area on the gaseous emissions, a model is being developed simulating the logistics of collective slurry management and the storage tank dynamics (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2007). Also, the effect of uncertainties related to key processes and assumptions in the environmental assessment of slurry management scenarios are part of our present endeavours.

In addition to the environmental impact categories evaluated in this study, it would be important for future studies to include aspects related to bio-security aspects of slurry management as well as nuisances (e.g. noise, odours) caused by transport and spreading of slurry and treatment by-products. Also, the reduction of slurry production by decreased regional herd size should be evaluated to describe the trade-offs between economic and envi- ronmental objectives. Finally, whatever methods used and scenarios analysed, the environmental analysis should help different actors involved in, or affected by, slurry management to discuss transparently and take informed decisions on how to tackle this important environmental challenge.
